_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 10-9 Suspected Pandemic (MDHHS Reviewed 2022-12-27). Base Michigan template.
condition: Suspected Pandemic — Acute Febrile Respiratory Illness (AFRI)
protocol_reference: Michigan Special Operations Protocol Section 10-9: Suspected Pandemic — MDHHS Reviewed 2022
purpose: Establish EMS infection control, patient management, and provider health guidelines for responding to patients with Acute Febrile Respiratory Illness (AFRI) during a suspected or declared pandemic.

sections.title: AFRI Definition
content: AFRI — Acute Febrile Respiratory Illness: acute onset illness characterized by:
— Fever (measured or subjective)
— One or more respiratory symptoms: cough, sore throat, runny nose, shortness of breath
— Onset within previous 10 days
AFRI is the surveillance definition used to identify potential pandemic influenza and other respiratory pathogens requiring enhanced EMS precautions
During declared or suspected pandemic: all patients meeting AFRI criteria receive enhanced precaution response

sections.title: MCA and Agency Recommendations — Baseline Pandemic Preparedness
content: MCAs and EMS agencies should pre-position and maintain supplies of:
— N-95 respirators (fit-tested) for EMS providers
— Isolation gowns / fluid-resistant gowns
— Gloves (non-latex, examination grade)
— Hand sanitizer / soap for all vehicles
— Surgical masks for patient placement
STAFF HEALTH: EMS providers who are ill (fever, respiratory symptoms) should NOT report to work — sick provider stay-home policy
Agencies should establish clear sick leave and return-to-work criteria in coordination with occupational health

sections.title: EMS Provider PPE — AFRI Patient Contact
content: Minimum PPE for AFRI patient contact:
— N-95 respirator (must be fit-tested — surgical mask acceptable as MINIMUM if N-95 unavailable)
— Eye protection (goggles or face shield) for procedures generating respiratory droplets
— Examination gloves
— Isolation gown for close patient contact
Don PPE BEFORE patient contact — do not don after entering patient environment
Doff PPE carefully after patient delivery — follow gloves-off sequence; hand hygiene immediately after doff
N-95 must be fit-tested per OSHA 1910.134 standards — annual fit testing required

sections.title: Patient Management — AFRI Suspected
content: 1. Don appropriate PPE before patient contact (N-95, gown, gloves, eye protection as needed)
2. Place surgical mask on PATIENT as early as possible — source control
3. Notify receiving hospital and medical control of AFRI/pandemic patient before arrival
4. Provide standard supportive care per applicable protocols (respiratory, cardiac, etc.)
5. Minimize number of personnel in patient care area — only essential providers in patient compartment
6. Ventilate ambulance: open partition window (if present), run exhaust fan, maximize fresh air circulation
7. Post-transport: perform enhanced cleaning and disinfection of ambulance per agency infection control protocols
8. EMS providers with significant AFRI patient exposure: follow post-exposure monitoring protocols below

sections.title: Ambulance Ventilation and Engineering Controls
content: Patient compartment: maximize air exchange — open vent windows, rear exhaust fan if equipped
Driver/patient compartment partition: keep closed (if present) to reduce droplet transmission to driver
HEPA filtration: use HEPA filter exhaust systems if vehicle-equipped
Post-transport: leave rear doors open and vehicle ventilating for minimum 15-30 minutes before cleaning
High-touch surfaces: wipe down with EPA-registered disinfectant before and after each AFRI transport

sections.title: Post-Exposure Guidelines
content: EMS provider with unprotected exposure (no N-95, patient coughed/sneezed in face, splash to mucous membranes):
— Report exposure to supervisor immediately
— File exposure report per agency protocol
— Contact occupational health or agency infection control officer
— Self-monitor for AFRI symptoms for 10 days post-exposure
— If fever or respiratory symptoms develop: do NOT work; contact supervisor and occupational health
Antiviral prophylaxis (e.g., oseltamivir): per occupational health guidance — NOT a prehospital EMS decision
Return to work criteria: per MCA/agency pandemic policy and occupational health guidance

sections.title: Special Pathogen — SPRN Escalation
content: If AFRI patient is suspected of being a HIGH-CONSEQUENCE infectious disease (Ebola, Marburg, SARS, novel highly pathogenic influenza):
— Standard AFRI precautions are INSUFFICIENT — escalate to SPRN protocols
— Contact online medical control and MDHHS immediately
— Refer to Protocols 10-10 through 10-18 (SPRN series)
Indicators for SPRN escalation:
— Recent travel to MDHHS-designated high-risk country with current outbreak
— Known contact with confirmed high-consequence infectious disease case
— Unexplained hemorrhagic symptoms with fever
— MDHHS or CDC outbreak advisory for specific pathogen

deterioration_flags: AFRI patient with hemorrhagic symptoms (bleeding from multiple sites, unexplained bruising) — escalate to SPRN protocols 10-10 through 10-18; contact MDHHS
EMS provider develops acute fever/respiratory symptoms within 10 days of AFRI patient exposure — stay home; contact supervisor; seek occupational health evaluation
scope_notes: This protocol applies to AFRI (Acute Febrile Respiratory Illness) during suspected or declared pandemic. For HIGH-CONSEQUENCE pathogens (Ebola-class), escalate to SPRN protocols 10-10 through 10-18. N-95 respirator use requires annual OSHA fit testing. Sick EMS providers should NOT report to work — this is a primary pandemic containment measure.
